one_line_definition: Rational polynomial in Laplace variable s describing filter output/input ratio; roots of numerator are zeros, roots of denominator are poles.
Root of the transfer function denominator; each pole contributes –20 dB/decade rolloff and 90° phase shift beyond the corner frequency.
Root of the transfer function numerator; each zero contributes +20 dB/decade and +90° phase shift.
Frequency at which the filter response is 3 dB below the passband level (or at the passband ripple edge for Chebyshev).
Ratio of resonant frequency to bandwidth; controls passband peaking (Q > 0.707 causes peaking in a 2-pole section).
α = 1/Q; determines how quickly transients decay and the shape of the filter rolloff.
Negative derivative of phase with respect to frequency (–dφ/dω); constant group delay implies linear phase and undistorted pulse transmission.
All-pole filter with no passband or stopband ripple; pole locations equally spaced on the unit circle in the s-plane.
All-pole filter with equiripple passband and monotonic stopband; poles on an ellipse derived from the Butterworth circle; sharper rolloff than Butterworth of same order.
All-pole filter optimised for maximally flat group delay (linear phase) in the passband; best transient response, worst amplitude discrimination.
Filter with ripple in both passband and stopband; finite-frequency zeros (from numerator) give the fastest possible transition from passband to stopband for a given order.
Filter with monotonic passband and equiripple stopband; derived by frequency-inverting a Chebyshev response.
Filter approximation extending linear phase into the stopband by allowing phase ripple; intermediate between Bessel and Chebyshev in amplitude vs. time domain trade-off.
Compromise between Gaussian (Bessel-like) and Chebyshev responses; nearly linear phase in passband with steeper stopband rolloff than Bessel beyond a breakpoint.
Filter with unity gain at all frequencies; provides controlled phase shift (0° to 360°) to equalise group delay of another filter without affecting amplitude.
Peak-to-peak variation of the filter amplitude response within the passband, expressed in dB.
Minimum signal attenuation guaranteed within the stopband, expressed in dB.
Degree of the transfer function denominator; equals the number of reactive elements (poles); each order adds –20 dB/decade of rolloff rate.
Normalised low-pass filter (cutoff at 1 rad/s, 1 Ω impedance) used as the starting point for designing any filter type via frequency transformations.

aliases_seen: LP to HP, LP to BP, LP to notch
one_line_definition: Mathematical substitution applied to the low-pass prototype transfer function to obtain high-pass, band-pass, or band-reject responses.
Algorithm for converting low-pass prototype complex pole pairs into equivalent band-pass filter sections with correct Q and resonant frequencies.
Two-pole active filter using op amp as voltage-controlled voltage source (amplifier); noninverting, low component spread, highest usable frequency for a given op amp.
Two-pole active filter using op amp as integrator; inverting; higher component spread; limited to moderate Q by op-amp gain-bandwidth product.
Active filter using multiple op amps in an integrator loop; simultaneous low-pass, band-pass, and high-pass outputs; good tuning flexibility.
Two-integrator loop active filter topology providing adjustable Q and F0 with low component sensitivity.
Two op-amp circuit whose input impedance Z = Z1·Z3·Z5 / (Z2·Z4); used to synthesise active inductors and FDNR elements.

aliases_seen: Frequency Dependent Negative Resistor, D element
one_line_definition: Impedance element with Z proportional to 1/s²; synthesised from GIC with two capacitors; enables passive LC filter realisation without physical inductors.
Inductor simulated using a GIC with one capacitor and resistors; avoids physical inductor parasitics.
Passive RC bridge circuit that produces a notch (null) at a specific frequency; used as the feedback network of an active notch filter.
Active notch filter topology with high Q and independently adjustable notch depth; uses two op amps.
Single op-amp notch filter topology offering low component count; separate low-pass and high-pass notch variants.
Active band-pass topology using two op amps; allows independent tuning of Q and F0.
Complex plane with real axis σ (Neper frequency) and imaginary axis jω (angular frequency); pole and zero locations on this plane determine filter stability and frequency response character.
Output of a filter to a unit impulse input; bandwidth of impulse response is inversely proportional to filter bandwidth; characterises filter speed.
Integral of the impulse response; characterised by rise time, overshoot, and ringing; useful for assessing envelope distortion in modulated signal systems.
Frequency range over which the filter transmits signals with acceptable attenuation (within Amax).
Frequency range over which the filter attenuates signals by at least Amin.
Filter passing a band of frequencies between lower and upper cutoff frequencies FL and FH; resonant frequency F0 is the geometric mean of FL and FH.
Filter that attenuates a narrow band of frequencies; Q determines the notch width; standard, low-pass, and high-pass notch variants exist depending on relative pole and zero frequencies.
